tax: Bog-Iron Bloomling is placed at species rank beneath Bloomling (Metalline order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a porous bog-iron body, magnetic mass-attack, and a bleeding rust trail. It is separated from its sibling the Native Copper Coil by its inert magnetic ferrous mass versus the Coil's conductive lashing ribbon; the two share the genus body-plan but never the electrical conduction. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: porous bog-iron and ferruginous mud cemented by earth-pressure.
ap: Bog-Iron Bloomling stands 1.5–2.2 m, a heavy lumpen mass. Its silhouette is a hunched spongy mound that hauls itself forward on iron pseudopods. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a rust-red, pitted sponge surface that bleeds orange in water; a strong magnetism that drags iron tools and fittings toward it; and a metallic, blood-like tang of iron on the air around it. It moves with a slow, heaving lurch, slowest of all the metalline kin, and shows none; recognised by rust-colour and magnetic pull. Even after dispersal it can be identified from an orange rust-bleed trail and iron filings clinging to nearby fittings, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
eco: Bog-Iron Bloomling persists in iron-stained Still-water bogs of Tier III, where ferruginous mud and standing water cement its porous body. It does not breed; it forms where earth-pressure cements a bog-iron bloom out of ferruginous mud, and recurs whenever the bog re-precipitates iron and the bloom re-cements. It sustains its mass on earth-pressure in iron-rich mud, drawing in bog-iron and ferruginous mud drawn in to heal and grow. Range is bounded by the iron-rich bog margin: outside that band the body dries, oxidises, and crumbles to rust away from the bog. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a bog patch where iron tools drift and the water runs orange without rain, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Bog-Iron Bloomling is roused by metal carried into its bog or heavy footfall and otherwise lies a rust-coloured mound posing as a bog-iron bank. It claims a single iron bog and answers intrusion with lurching to ram and pin with magnetic mass. Toward grey-folk it is slow but inexorable; survey crews report that Forgemasters strip iron fittings before crossing an orange-bleeding bog. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
traitsText: Stable characters of Bog-Iron Bloomling: a body of porous bog-iron and ferruginous mud cemented by earth-pressure; immune to piercing and to magnetism-based effects; and a defining weakness — acid, which dissolves its porous iron, and fire-drying. Its senses run through the medium itself: magnetic and pressure sensing; blind to non-ferrous, non-moving objects. It tolerates cold, damp, and darkness but fails at acid seep and dry heat. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the porous mound and magnetic mass, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
abilities: Abilities of Bog-Iron Bloomling are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it rams with crushing ferrous mass to pin prey; it drags iron tools and armour to itself magnetically; it draws bog-iron in to seal damage; and it sinks into the bog to ambush and resurface. Each is a direct expression of earth-pressure in a heavy magnetic iron body, so it cannot, for instance, conduct charge, move fast, or cross dry ground. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
bg: In the Throat, Bog-Iron Bloomling matters because it makes iron bogs a magnetic trap that disarms crews, who now strip ferrous gear before crossing. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a magnetic mass hazard logged with iron bogs, and its presence reshapes routes through the Tier III iron bogs, now crossed stripped of ferrous fittings. An orange bleed with no rain, and tools that drift bog-ward, mark a forming Bloomling.
g: Bog-Iron Bloomling (Metallum ferrugineum) is a metalline elemental of porous bog-iron that lumbers as a rust-bleeding mass and rams with sheer ferrous weight. Documented from the iron-stained Still-water bogs of Tier III, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a spongy, rust-red mass of porous bog-iron and ferruginous mud — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Where the Copper Coil conducts and lashes, the Bloomling is inert, magnetic, and crushingly heavy. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a slumped pile of rust-red iron sponge bleeding orange into the water and no carcass.
